![](data:image/png;base64,)

Advantages of NN

NN can be efficient after it's trained.

Can continue to learn if given new training data

Disadvantages

Can be hardware dependent

Processing power - particularly when training,

Black box

Approximate results

Only as good as data used to train it

Convolutional NN is good for processing images, e.g. detect cell types

CNN used to automatically label maps. Use labelled data to train it, so it can detect roads, etc.

[https://livenorthumbriaac-my.sharepoint.com/:f:/g/personal/anne_james_northumbria_ac_uk/EgdGDePVGkhNjXg8JihKQiYBLBZ70OTXHyk4mfarbxKhAg?e=EBKQYj](https://livenorthumbriaac-my.sharepoint.com/:f:/g/personal/anne_james_northumbria_ac_uk/EgdGDePVGkhNjXg8JihKQiYBLBZ70OTXHyk4mfarbxKhAg?e=EBKQYj)

For simple NN need to flatten image into 1d

For CNN can keep as 2d

Tensorflow and keras are libraries for NN

Keras is a nice user firendly front end to tensorflow

Batch_size relates to when weights updated, too small and it will take much longer

[https://machinelearningmastery.com/choose-an-activation-function-for-deep-learning](https://machinelearningmastery.com/choose-an-activation-function-for-deep-learning)

ReLu doesn't normalise, but seems to work well with NN. It works well stopping stuff going to zero, but potential risk of exploding.

Possible that you have too many epochs and performance may dip

Regularisation, helps prevent it overfitting, adds some error to make it work harder to be more general.

e.g. if training is 0.9 but test is 0.6 suggest it might be overfitted.

Dropout only acts in training, not prediction
